q: Which component is used to 'open' or 'close' a circuit?
sol: A switch or key is the component that starts or stops the flow of current.

q: In the symbol of an electric cell, the longer line represents:
sol: The long thin line is the positive terminal and the short thick line is the negative terminal.

q: A combination of two or more cells is called a:
sol: Connecting multiple cells together forms a battery.

q: Which effect of current is used in an electric heater?
sol: Heating effect occurs when current flows through a high-resistance wire like Nichrome.

q: The thin wire inside a bulb that glows is called the:
sol: The filament (usually made of tungsten) gets white-hot and emits light.

q: A safety device used to prevent damage to electrical circuits is a:
sol: A fuse melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes dangerously high.

q: Who discovered the magnetic effect of electric current?
sol: Oersted noticed that a compass needle deflects when current flows nearby.

q: A coil of insulated wire wrapped around an iron piece acts as an:
sol: When current passes through the coil, the iron becomes magnetic.

q: Electric current flows only when the circuit is:
sol: A 'closed' circuit provides a continuous path for electrons to move.

q: MCB stands for:
sol: MCBs are modern switches that automatically turn off during an overload.

q: What happens to a compass needle kept near a wire when current is switched on?
sol: The magnetic field produced by the current affects the compass.

q: Which material is used for making the 'element' of an electric iron?
sol: Nichrome is used because it has high resistance and a high melting point.

q: When the filament of a bulb breaks, the bulb is said to be:
sol: A broken filament means the circuit is incomplete; the bulb won't glow.

q: If cells are connected in series, the positive terminal of one cell is connected to:
sol: For current to flow, terminals must be connected in alternating fashion (+ to -).

q: An electric bell works on the principle of:
sol: It uses an electromagnet to pull a hammer towards a gong.

q: Which of these is a source of electric current?
sol: A cell converts chemical energy into electrical energy.

q: Why is a fuse wire made of a material with a low melting point?
o: So it can carry more current
So it melts easily during overload
sol: Safety depends on the wire melting quickly when heat exceeds safe limits.

q: CFL stands for:
sol: CFLs are energy-efficient alternatives to traditional bulbs.

q: A 'closed circuit' is also called an ________ circuit.
sol: Complete means the path for electricity is not interrupted.

q: Which of the following is NOT a component of a simple circuit?
sol: A basic circuit needs a source, path, and switch; a magnet is an extra/separate tool.

q: ISI mark on electrical appliances ensures:
sol: ISI mark is a standard certification for safety in India.

q: If the switch is in the 'OFF' position, the circuit is:
sol: OFF position creates a gap in the path, making it an open circuit.

q: Which part of the electric bell attracts the iron strip?
sol: The electromagnet creates the force that pulls the iron strip.

q: Connecting too many appliances to a single socket can cause:
sol: Overloading happens when the current drawn exceeds the limit of the wire.

q: What happens to an electromagnet when the current is switched OFF?
sol: Temporary magnetism is a key feature of electromagnets.

q: Which device uses the heating effect of current?
sol: Kettles use heating elements to boil water.

q: The short thick line in a cell symbol is the:
sol: Standard convention uses the thicker, shorter line for negative.

q: In a battery of 4 cells, how many positive terminals are there?
sol: Each cell has one positive and one negative terminal.

q: The amount of heat produced in a wire depends on its:
sol: Resistance and heat are influenced by material, dimensions, and time.

q: Which of these is better than a fuse for home safety?
sol: MCBs are more convenient as they can be reset without replacement.

q: Electromagnets are used in:
sol: Electromagnets are found in various industrial and household devices.

q: If one bulb in a series circuit fuses, the other bulbs will:
sol: In a series circuit, if one part breaks, the whole circuit stops.

q: A fuse is always connected in ________ with the circuit.
sol: Fuses must be in series so all current passes through them.

q: Which gas is filled in fluorescent tubes to make them glow?
sol: Mercury vapor helps in producing UV light which turns into visible light.

q: What is the reason for a short circuit?
o: Touching of live and neutral wires
sol: Direct contact between wires bypasses the load and creates high current.

q: An electric cell converts ________ energy into electrical energy.
sol: Chemicals inside the cell react to produce electricity.

q: When a switch is in 'ON' position, the circuit is:
sol: ON position connects the wires, making a closed loop.

q: Which of these conducts electricity?
sol: Copper is a good conductor of electricity.

q: The flow of electricity is known as:
sol: Electric current is the movement of charge through a conductor.

q: A coil of wire in a heater is called:
sol: The 'element' is the part that generates heat.

q: Why do we use LED bulbs nowadays?
sol: LEDs are extremely energy-efficient compared to traditional bulbs.

q: Which part of an electric bell hits the gong?
sol: The hammer is the moving part that creates the sound.

q: In an electric circuit, the arrow indicates the direction of:
sol: Current direction is usually shown from positive to negative terminal.

q: If we increase the number of turns in an electromagnet, its strength:
sol: More turns create a stronger magnetic field.

q: An electromagnet is a ________ magnet.
sol: It only works as long as current is flowing.

q: Which device does NOT work on the magnetic effect of current?
sol: Toasters work on the heating effect of current.

q: What happens when you bring a magnet near a current-carrying wire?
o: The wire moves or feels a force
sol: The two magnetic fields interact with each other.

q: A cell symbol has a small '+' sign near which line?
sol: The long line is the positive side.

q: Electricity generated from a wall socket is:
sol: Mains supply in houses is AC.

q: Fuses are often made of an alloy of:
sol: Tin-lead alloy has the required low melting point.

q: Why shouldn't you touch a glowing bulb with your hand?
sol: Incandescent bulbs produce a lot of heat along with light.

q: How can we make an electromagnet stronger?
sol: All these factors boost the magnetic flux.

q: Which symbol represents a group of cells?
o: Two parallel lines of same length
sol: A battery symbol shows multiple cells in a row.

q: What protects a circuit from fire due to short circuit?
sol: The fuse breaks the circuit before the heat can cause a fire.

q: Electric energy is measured in terms of:
sol: Our electricity bills are based on kWh consumed.

q: When current flows through a wire, the wire gets:
sol: Heat is produced due to the resistance of the wire.

q: The soft iron piece in an electric bell is attached to a:
sol: The spring pulls the iron strip back when magnetism is lost.

q: Which of these is used in torches?
sol: Torches use cells to provide portable power.

q: A fuse wire should have ________ resistance and ________ melting point.
sol: High resistance generates heat quickly, and low melting point breaks the circuit.

q: Which material is a poor conductor of electricity?
sol: Plastic does not allow current to pass through it.

q: Who invented the first electric bulb?
sol: Edison is credited with the commercial success of the light bulb.

q: If you add more bulbs in a series circuit, they will:
sol: Voltage is shared, so each bulb gets less power.

q: Which of these devices contains an electromagnet?
sol: Electromagnets are used in motors, seals, and data storage.

q: What is the purpose of the 'contact screw' in an electric bell?
o: To fix the bell to wall
To complete and break the circuit
sol: The screw makes/breaks contact with the moving iron strip to cause vibration.

q: Copper wires used in circuits are covered with plastic to:
sol: Plastic is an insulator that protects users from current.

q: A standard cell has a voltage of approximately:
sol: Common dry cells used in remotes/torches are 1.5 volts.

q: Heating effect of current is ________ if the wire is thicker (lower resistance).
sol: Lower resistance leads to less heat generation for the same current.

q: In which position can the switch be placed in a circuit?
sol: As long as it breaks the loop, its location doesn't matter much.

q: What happens if a fuse is replaced by a thick copper wire?
o: Circuit might catch fire during overload
sol: Copper won't melt easily, allowing dangerous current to flow.

q: Which component is represented by a circle with a cross (X) inside?
sol: This is a standard symbol for a light bulb or lamp.

q: The core of an electromagnet should be made of:
sol: Soft iron magnetizes and demagnetizes quickly.

q: Electric heaters are dangerous to use near:
sol: Flammable materials can catch fire from the heat.

q: Why do some wires get very hot while others don't?
sol: High resistance wires (like Nichrome) get hotter than low resistance ones (like Copper).

q: When a switch is 'ON', the circuit is from ________ to ________.
sol: Conventional current flows from positive to negative.

q: Which of these is a safety precaution while handling electricity?
sol: Rubber is an insulator and prevents shocks.

q: An electric circuit is a ________ path.
sol: Electricity needs a complete, closed loop to flow.

q: Magnetic effect of current is used in which of these?
sol: Cranes use electromagnets to lift heavy iron objects.

q: What is the result of connecting cells in parallel?
sol: Parallel connection keeps voltage same but increases capacity.

q: A magnet made using electricity is called:
sol: The name 'electro-magnet' implies it uses electricity.

q: The property of a material to oppose the flow of current is:
sol: High resistance makes it harder for current to flow.

q: Which light source is most energy efficient?
sol: LEDs use the least power for the same amount of light.

q: A dry cell produces ________ electricity.
sol: Cells and batteries provide Direct Current.

q: What is the color of the 'live' wire in modern household wiring?
sol: Live wires are usually red or brown.

q: Why is tungsten used for bulb filaments?
o: It has a very high melting point
sol: Tungsten can withstand temperatures up to 3400°C without melting.

q: When current is too high, the fuse wire ________.
sol: Melting breaks the circuit and stops the current.

q: Which device 'makes' or 'breaks' the circuit?
sol: This is the primary function of a switch.

q: Magnetic field around a wire exists ________.
sol: No current means no magnetic field.

q: The symbol for a switch in 'ON' position shows:
sol: The connecting line indicates the path is closed.

q: Which of these is a danger of electricity?
sol: Electricity must be handled with great care.

q: A solenoid is a:
sol: A solenoid is a long coil of wire that acts like a magnet.

q: Which part of the cell is positive?
sol: In a standard dry cell, the top cap is positive.

q: In a torch, cells are usually placed ________.
sol: Series connection is needed to get enough voltage for the bulb.

q: Which component protects us from 'leakage' of current?
sol: Earthing sends leaked current safely to the ground.

q: The 'strength' of a cell is its:
sol: Voltage (V) determines how much 'push' it gives to the current.

q: What happens to the iron core when current in an electromagnet is reversed?
sol: Reversing current reverses the direction of the magnetic field.

q: Which material is used for making standard connecting wires?
sol: Copper is cheap, flexible, and a great conductor.

q: If a circuit has no battery, can current flow?
sol: A power source is mandatory for current to flow.

q: The 'humming' sound in transformers is due to:
sol: Magnetic forces cause parts of the transformer to vibrate.

q: A circuit diagram uses ________ to represent components.
sol: Symbols make it easy to draw and understand complex circuits.

q: Safety fuses should NOT be replaced by:
sol: Ordinary wires might not melt, creating a fire hazard.
